steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Nunez and colleagues claim that mapping time onto a front/back axis is 'not in fact ubiquitous.' The question asks which finding best supports this claim — meaning evidence that some group's temporal gestures are NOT anchored to a fixed front/back axis.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — unrelated gesture habits: describes how Yupno speakers gesture in other contexts, not temporal gestures tied to a front/back axis. ✗
  • Choice B — Yupno speakers gesture ahead when facing uphill, behind when facing downhill': shows their temporal gestures shift with terrain orientation, meaning no fixed front/back axis governs them — directly supporting the claim that this mapping is not universal. ✓
  • Choice C — indoor/outdoor frequency': notes when temporal gestures occur, not whether they follow a consistent front/back axis. ✗
  • Choice D — grammatical similarity to Spanish': addresses language structure, not gestural or spatial mapping of time. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
